title: Co-Parent Alignment Monthly Meeting
thesis: ND kids cannot afford two different rule systems. The 30-minute monthly meeting that prevents the slow drift into "Dad means consequences and Mom means safety" — and the three minimum-viable agreements that hold even when philosophical alignment doesn't.
meta_description: ND kids need consistent rules across caregivers. The 30-minute monthly meeting agenda + the three Minimum Viable Agreements that hold even when full alignment doesn't. Sourced from Survival Blueprint Ch. 5.
og_description: ND kids cannot afford two different rule systems. The monthly meeting that holds it together.
twitter_description: 30 minutes a month. Six items. The agenda that prevents the slow drift.
schema_keywords: co-parenting ND, ADHD parenting consistency, divorced parenting, IEP coordination, Survival Blueprint

pull_quote.text: In families raising a neurodivergent child, co-parent conflict about discipline, medication, and screen time produces a child who learns 'Dad means punishment and Mom means safety.' The monthly co-parent alignment meeting prevents the cycle.

sections.text: You and your co-parent agree your kid needs consistent rules. You disagree on what those rules are, when they apply, what counts as a violation, what the consequence should be, and whether the medication is helping. Each of you is right about half of it. Your kid lives the difference.

ND brains depend on predictability more than neurotypical brains do. Two rule systems isn't twice the structure — it's no structure. The child stops trying to predict the environment and starts running parent-to-parent triage: which one is here, what mood, ask for what. That's not manipulation. That's adaptation to chaos.

The Survival Blueprint monthly co-parent alignment meeting is a 30-minute structured agenda that prevents drift, surfaces problems early, and (when full philosophical alignment is impossible) lands on the Minimum Viable Agreement — three commitments that have to hold even when everything else is unresolved.
Three things make the meeting work.

sections.rest: The meeting is a calendar appointment, not an in-the-moment crisis response. 30 minutes, once a month, never during bedtime, never during a meltdown, never when either parent is angry. Ideally outside the house — coffee shop, parked car, a walk. The context is what allows the conversation to happen.

Six agenda items, in order: wins, data review, what's working, what's not, one focus for next month, self-care check. Rules: no interrupting, no phones, no relitigating past disagreements. If you can't get through the agenda without fighting, bring a therapist as moderator. That's not failure; that's wisdom.

sections.lead: Minimum Viable Agreement when alignment fails.
sections.rest: Most co-parents will not achieve full philosophical alignment. The fallback isn't endless argument — it's three non-negotiables that hold regardless of philosophical disagreement: never punish brain-based symptoms, present a united front in front of the child, protect the parent-child relationship over any single rule. Get those three locked and a lot of disagreement becomes survivable.

sections.label: THE STRUCTURE
sections.cite: Survival Blueprint Ch. 5 — the standing monthly meeting + the three Minimum Viable Agreements; the Tool 3 agenda template is reproduced on this page.

sections.text: Five steps. The first is the meeting structure; the last is the fallback when full alignment isn't achievable.

steps.name: Schedule the meeting — same time every month
steps.body: Pick a recurring slot and make it sacred. Saturday morning at 9 AM works for many families. Out of the house is better than in. No phones. No kids. No checking work email mid-meeting. If a month gets skipped, the next one runs the same agenda — don't double up.
steps.tip: If your relationship has high conflict, run the first three meetings with a therapist or trusted neutral. The structure is what you're learning, not the content.

steps.name: Run the 6-item agenda — 5 minutes each
steps.body: Wins (each parent names one thing the child did well + one thing the other parent did well). Data review (school reports, grades, medication side effects — facts, not feelings). What's working (continue). What's not (stop or modify, no blame). One focus for next month (single behavioral or academic goal, both parents commit to the SAME approach). Self-care check (how each parent is doing).
steps.tip: 5 minutes per item is a maximum, not a minimum. If you finish item 2 in 90 seconds, move on. The cap on time prevents any single item from swallowing the meeting.

steps.name: Lock one focus — not five
steps.body: The single most common failure of co-parent meetings is leaving with five new commitments instead of one. Pick one. The one with the highest leverage. The other four problems still exist; they wait until next month. Concentration is what makes the protocol work.
steps.tip: When in doubt, pick the focus that protects the parent-child relationship most directly. If a child is currently terrified of homework, fix homework before fixing screen time.

steps.name: Lock the three Minimum Viable Agreements
steps.body: These three hold even when philosophical alignment doesn't. (1) We will not punish brain-based symptoms — fidgeting, forgetting, time blindness, struggle to start, are symptoms not defiance. (2) We will present a united front — disagreements about approach are discussed privately, never in front of the child; neither parent overrides the other in real time. (3) We will protect the relationship first — if any strategy is consistently damaging the parent-child bond, we modify it even if we believe it "should" work.
steps.tip: Print the three Minimum Viable Agreements and post them where you both see them daily. They're load-bearing. Disagreement on item 1, 2, or 3 is the place that needs a therapist's help; everything else can be negotiated.

steps.name: Adapt for separated households
steps.body: Co-parents in separate households face exponentially harder consistency. Two solutions: written rule cards that travel with the child between houses (laminated, in the backpack), and a shared digital document for medication, school, and behavior tracking that both parents update. The meeting still happens — phone, video, neutral location, whatever works. The format is what makes it survivable.
steps.tip: When separated co-parents disagree on diagnosis or medication, the Survival Blueprint Ch. 5 is explicit: get the child's clinician involved, not the family lawyer. The clinician outranks both parents on this specific question.

after_steps_p: Print this. Use it as the actual agenda. Bring it to every meeting until the structure is automatic.

items.text: One thing the child did well + one thing the other parent did well.
items.help: Non-negotiable even when the month was terrible.

items.text: School reports, grades, medication side effects. Facts.
Strategies that produced positive results. Continue.
items.help: Name them explicitly so they don't drift.

items.text: ONE goal for next month. Then how each parent is actually doing.
items.help: Not five goals. One. Both parents same approach.

exits.title: The Survival Blueprint
exits.body: Chapter 5 covers the four co-parent archetypes (with conflict-pattern matrices), the full Tool 3 agenda template, and Section 5.4 on co-parenting after separation or divorce. Plus the three Minimum Viable Agreements in their full form.

exits.title: Validate-Then-Redirect OS
exits.body: The shared in-the-moment protocol both parents can run identically. Half of co-parent alignment is making sure the same script gets used in both households.

exits.title: The Live Show
exits.body: A live call-in show on the topics this wiki covers — unscripted, judgment-free, ordinary people, extraordinary stories. Join the waitlist for the launch.

sources_intro: All claims on this page are sourced from The Survival Blueprint, Chapter 5. Primary sources cited:
sources_list: Survival Blueprint Ch. 5 — The Co-Parent Alignment System; Tool 3 (Monthly Meeting Agenda); the three Minimum Viable Agreements.
Pruett, K. D. & Pruett, M. K. (2009). Partnership Parenting. Foundational on consistency across caregivers as a primary moderator of child outcomes.
Survival Blueprint Ch. 5.4 — Co-Parenting After Separation or Divorce.
